predecessor: baseline-round2-2026-07-20.json (round 2, against ac5be59, before the Vinstrið fix and before this round's chart-canonicalization/batch-reliability fixes)
method: Started from real batch operations (a --all --limit 10 fetch) rather than hand-picking articles, specifically to stress-test at a scale round 1/2 hadn't tried. Two significant, previously-undiscovered bugs surfaced directly from that one batch run before any questions were even drafted: a data-loss crash and a silent data-inconsistency bug. Both fixed and re-verified this round, then the 30 questions were built to document and cross-check the fixes plus probe adjacent territory.
headline_findings: CRITICAL bug found and fixed: a single article's Playwright timeout (verified: a genuinely slow/stuck RÚV page, reproduced twice) crashed the entire --all batch with an uncaught exception, losing every already-fetched article's data — the processed CSV was never written at all, even though 9 of 10 articles had already succeeded. Per-article try/except added; re-ran the identical failing batch afterward and it completed cleanly, writing 46 rows with a clear '1 article(s) failed' summary instead of losing everything.

SIGNIFICANT bug found and fixed: chart-extracted party names (RÚV Highcharts aria-labels, Vísir's equivalent) were never canonicalized through _PARTY_RE at all — only the prose path was. Real fetched data had 'Samfylking' AND 'Samfylkingin', 'Sósíalistaflokkur' AND 'Sósíalistaflokkur Íslands', 'Vinstri græn' AND the full legal name 'Vinstrihreyfingin - grænt framboð' as apparently-different parties across different articles. This would have silently broken any cross-article aggregation (exactly the 'trend'/'polling average' feature named as the highest-value gap in both prior rounds) by splitting one party into several rows keyed on whichever label string that article's chart happened to use. Fixed with _canonicalize_chart_party(), which also explicitly drops the 'Önnur framboð' (other lists) catch-all bucket rather than treating it as a party, and surfaces genuinely-unrecognized labels in the skip log instead of silently passing them through.

Two real minor-party/regex gaps found and fixed as a direct consequence of the canonicalization work: 'Lýðræðisflokkur' (Arnar Þór Jónsson's party) was missing from _PARTY_STEMS entirely despite appearing in two independent real fetches; the Vinstri græn regex didn't match the party's own full legal name.

Cross-article aggregation now genuinely works: a real duckdb query (min/max fylgi per party across 5 fetched articles) returned correct, consistent results for the first time this session — this was 'unable' in both prior rounds specifically because the underlying data wasn't consistently keyed.

results.answer: No, not anymore — verified fixed. Before this round's fix, yes (Samfylking/Samfylkingin diverged); after, both canonicalize to 'Samfylking'.

Yes — 'Sósíalistaflokkur Íslands' correctly canonicalizes to 'Sósíalistaflokkur' (the existing stem already substring-matches it; the bug was purely that the chart path never ran canonicalization at all, not that the regex itself needed changing for this one).
Yes — verified: ruv-467932 dropped from 10 raw chart labels to 9 canonicalized party rows, the missing one being 'Önnur framboð' (0.0%), correctly excluded.

Before this round's fix: yes, the whole batch's data was lost (see headline finding). After: no — verified by re-running the identical failing batch, which completed with 46 rows written and 1 article cleanly reported as failed.

Yes, always were — each article's raw {id}.json is written inside the per-article loop iteration, immediately after a successful fetch, independent of whether a later article in the same batch fails. Confirmed: after the original crash, 9 of 10 articles' raw JSON files existed on disk even though the aggregated CSV did not.

~85 seconds for 10 articles (9 RÚV + implicitly capped list ordering meant no Vísir in this particular batch), including one 60-second timeout — so roughly 3 seconds/article for the 9 that succeeded normally, dominated by Playwright browser launches.

results.gap: Absence of evidence isn't proof of absence — the collision risk documented in the code comment (sentence-initial capitalization of the generic word) hasn't been stress-tested against a large enough sample of Reykjavík-scope articles specifically, where both usages would actually compete.

results.answer: 5 articles with fully consistent, canonicalized numbers in the current processed CSV as of this round's final state (all chart-sourced, from the --all batch run) — plus 4 more verified-but-not-currently-in-the-same-CSV-snapshot from the regression checks (451831, 428434, and two Vísir prose articles), since the CSV was cleared between test runs this round rather than accumulated.

From round 1's list: 'Build Vísir fetch' — done (round 2). From round 2's list: the 'Vinstrið' scoping decision — done (this session, pre-round-3, via Wikipedia research); the 'er með' verb addition — still open (Q15); the methodology-field extractor — still open (Q20).

The methodology-field gap (sample size, field dates, response rate all prose-only, never structured) is now the largest standing gap that's been independently confirmed unchanged across all three rounds — everything else flagged in round 1 or 2 has since been either fixed, explained, or narrowed to a specific untested-but-scoped follow-up (Heimildin query broadening, VB discovery, the 'er með' verb, the Q14/Q17 unverified-absence gaps).

gap_patterns_round3: The two bugs this round both came from *scale*, not from a new article's prose wording — round 1/2 found language-level gaps by reading individual articles closely; round 3 found a data-consistency bug and a reliability bug by running a batch of 10 and actually inspecting the aggregated output, a different failure mode entirely. Future rounds should keep alternating between close-reading single articles and batch/aggregate stress-testing — each finds bugs the other can't.

Both fixes this round were 'silent' failure modes (wrong-but-plausible data, or total data loss with no error surfaced to the CSV) rather than 'loud' ones (a crash the user would immediately notice) — worth remembering that this class of bug is disproportionately dangerous precisely because nothing looked wrong until someone queried across articles and got inconsistent results.
highest_value_next_steps: The methodology-field extractor (sample size, field dates, response rate) is now the single most-repeated open item across all three rounds — highest-confidence next build.
Decide on 'er með' as a fourth poll-cue construction — same shape as the 'úr X í Y' fix already made, likely similarly low-risk once 2-3 more real examples are checked.
A systematic chart-vs-prose completeness check (round 3's Q17) — confirm chart extraction isn't silently missing a party the article's own prose discusses, on a sample large enough to trust.
